title: Rh Alloimmunization
chief_complaint: I'm 26 weeks pregnant. My antibody screen is positive. I'm Rh-negative and my husband is Rh-positive. I didn't get Rhogam after my first delivery because I didn't know I needed it.

physical_exam.Obstetric: Fundal height 26cm. Fetal HR 145. Normal uterine tone.

hidden_data.antibody: Anti-D antibody titer 1:256 (critical titer - >1:16 requires further evaluation). Father: Rh-positive (homozygous - all pregnancies will be Rh-positive).
hidden_data.mca_doppler: MCA peak systolic velocity 1.8 MoM (>1.5 MoM indicates severe fetal anemia - this fetus is severely anemic).
hidden_data.ob_us: Fetal hydrops: Scalp edema, Ascites, Pleural effusion, Cardiomegaly. Hepatosplenomegaly. Placenta thickened (placentomegaly). Polyhydramnios. BPP 6/8.
hidden_data.cordocentesis: Percutaneous umbilical blood sampling: Fetal Hb 5.5 g/dL (severe anemia - normal 15). Reticulocyte count elevated (compensatory). Direct Coombs test positive.
hidden_data.amniocentesis: Amniotic fluid: Bilirubin delta OD450 in zone 3 (Liley curve - severe anemia, impending demise).

correct_diagnosis: Severe Rh(D) Alloimmunization with Fetal Hydrops and Severe Anemia (Hb 5.5) at 26 Weeks
key_learning_points: Rh alloimmunization = Rh-negative mother produces anti-D IgG antibodies → cross placenta → attack Rh-positive fetal RBCs → fetal hemolytic anemia → hydrops → death. PREVENTABLE with Rhogam (anti-D immunoglobulin)

Pathophysiology: Sensitizing event (delivery, miscarriage, abortion, ectopic, trauma, APH, ECV, amniocentesis, CVS) → fetomaternal hemorrhage → maternal immune response → anti-D IgG (permanent). First Rh-positive pregnancy usually not affected (IgM primary response - does not cross placenta). Subsequent pregnancies: IgG crosses placenta → fetal anemia

Prevention: Rhogam 300mcg IM at 28 weeks (antenatal prophylaxis) + Within 72h of delivery (postpartum prophylaxis) if newborn Rh-positive. Also after any sensitizing event. Kleihauer-Betke test if large fetomaternal hemorrhage suspected (quantifies fetal cells in maternal circulation, determines if additional Rhogam needed). This prevents 99% of Rh alloimmunization

Monitoring alloimmunized pregnancy: Serial antibody titers q2-4 weeks. If critical titer (>1:16 for anti-D): MCA Doppler weekly from 16-18 weeks. MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM = severe fetal anemia (sensitivity 100%, false positive 12%). Cordocentesis/PUBS if MCA Doppler abnormal or hydrops present. Amniocentesis for delta OD450 (Liley curve) less commonly used now

Treatment: INTRAUTERINE TRANSFUSION (IUT) - US-guided transfusion of O-negative, irradiated, CMV-negative, leukoreduced pRBCs into umbilical vein. Corrects anemia, reverses hydrops. Repeat q2-4 weeks until delivery (transfused RBCs are adult Hb - released oxygen normally). Delivery at 34-37 weeks after lung maturity. Neonate: Phototherapy for hyperbilirubinemia, Exchange transfusion if severe, Top-up transfusions for anemia. Monitor for late anemia (bone marrow suppression from IUT + ongoing hemolysis)
Prognosis: Without treatment: Perinatal mortality 50% (hydrops). With IUT: Survival >90% (non-hydropic), 70-80% (hydropic). Neurodevelopment normal in 95% of survivors
